evidence_quotes.quote: A seller must never be allowed to restrict my access to a product I've already purchased.
such as the ability to sell books or audiobooks without Digital Rights Management (DRM).
If I buy an eBook, I should be able to read it on any device.

paragraphs.text: Digital fairness goes beyond not being tricked by technology companies and should include permanent access to products that consumers purchased, and the ability to own and use the products at the consumers discretion (within the bounds of existing laws). * If I buy an eBook, I should be able to read it on any device. If the seller stops selling it, I get to keep it, and read it wherever I want. A seller must never be allowed to restrict my access to a product I've already purchased. Amazon's is particularly egregious in this sector. Fairness is also not just a question of consumer rights, but author's rights, too, such as the ability to sell books or audiobooks without Digital Rights Management (DRM). * If I buy a video game, the seller must make it available even beyond the period where they make it available for purchase. The Stop Killing Games initiative (1.4 million signatures) outlines steps to make this a minimal inconvenience for publishers while delivering huge wins for consumers. Many independent publishers and developers support it due to its design, only large, profit-hungry publishers aim to minimise their obligations while raking in profit. * Movies & TV Shows that I've purchased on any digital store must be available to me indefinitely, unless explicitly sold as rentals. Once sold, it cannot be revoked and if the seller wants to stop offering it for download they must inform each owner and give them the opportunity to download it before it gets taken offline. Similar to books, this must include the ability to play the media at the consumer's discretion. A lack of regulation in the technology spaces has enabled some of the most egregious anti-consumer behaviours that we would not stand for in the physical world. Please ensure that corporate profits don't come before citizen's rights. The first priority always needs to be what value a business offers to society, not to shareholders.
